coredata.dc:title: Mn(II)-coordinated Fluorescent Carbon Dots: Preparation and Discrimination of Organic Solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yuru
Wang, Tianren
Chen, Xi
Xu, Yang
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we prepared a Mn(II)-coordinated carbon dots (CDs) with fluorescence and MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) bimodal properties by a one-pot solvothermal method and separated via silica column chromatography. The quantum yield of the CDs increased greatly from 2.27% to 6.75% with increase of Mn(II) doping, meanwhile the CDs exhibited a higher MR activity (7.28 mM−1s−1) than that of commercial Gd-DTPA (4.63 mM−1s−1). In addition, white light emitting CDs were obtained by mixing the different types of CDs. Notably, these CDs exhibited different fluorescence emissions in different organic solvents and could be used to discriminate organic solvents based on the polarity and protonation of the solvents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn(II)-coordinated carbon dots
Fluorescence enhancement
Magnetic resonance
White light emitting
Discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of Mn(II)-coordinated CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of (A) CDs-1, (B) CDs-2, (C) CDs-3 and (D) CDs-4, respectively. Inset: photographs of CDs solutions under (a) visible light and (b) a UV light at 302 nm.
(A–D) TEM images and (E–H) size distributions of the four samples. Inset: HRTEM images of the four samples. The scale bars in the insets represent 1 nm.
(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4. (B) XPS spectra of Mn2p in CD-2.
The r1 relaxivity curves of (A) CDs-2 and (B) Gd-DTPA.
(A) The fluorescence spectrum and (B) the fluorescence intensities plots of as-prepared samples produced by adding different amounts of Mn(II). (C) Measurements of QYs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of a mixture of the four types of CDs under UV irradiation, λ = 302 nm. Inset: Photograph of white-light fluorescence emission from CDs. (B) CIE coordinates as calculated from the fluorescence spectra of the CDs.

(A) Photographs of the same amounts of white-light emitting mixed CDs dissolved in different organic solvents under UV irradiation (λ = 302 nm). Solvents from left to right: PET, DCM, EAC, THF, NBA, NPA, EtOH, MeOH, and DMF. (B) Photographs of (a) mixed white-light emitting CDs in a developing agent. The same amount of mixed white-light emitting CDs were dissolved in (b) DCM, (c) DMF, and (d) EtOH. (C) Fluorescence spectra of mixed CDs were dissolved in different organic solvents.
Schematic representation of Mn-CDs for the discrimination of organic solvents.

head.ce:title: Mn(II)-coordinated Fluorescent Carbon Dots: Preparation and Discrimination of Organic Solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin 300130 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we prepared a Mn(II)-coordinated carbon dots (CDs) with fluorescence and MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) bimodal properties by a one-pot solvothermal method and separated via silica column chromatography. The quantum yield of the CDs increased greatly from 2.27% to 6.75% with increase of Mn(II) doping, meanwhile the CDs exhibited a higher MR activity (7.28 mM−1s−1) than that of commercial Gd-DTPA (4.63 mM−1s−1). In addition, white light emitting CDs were obtained by mixing the different types of CDs. Notably, these CDs exhibited different fluorescence emissions in different organic solvents and could be used to discriminate organic solvents based on the polarity and protonation of the solvents.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention due to their various fascinating advantages, which include tunable fluorescence, excellent photo- and chemical-stability, low toxicity, and favorable biocompatibility [1–5], which have been applied in biological sensing and imaging [6–11]. However, their usage remains challenging due to few methods available for fluorescence enhancement and functionalization [12].

Metal-enhanced fluorescence has emerged as an efficient way to improve the fluorescence properties of CDs. Zhang et al. found that the fluorescence of CDs could be enhanced via a metal-enhanced fluorescence process using silver island films [13]. CDs with excellent fluorescence can also be obtained by doping with ZnO or ZnS, as shown by Sun et al. [14]. Wang et al. prepared Mn(II)-coordinated CDs that exhibited yellow emission and could be used for the discrimination of volatile organic compounds [15]. Thus, metal-enhanced fluorescence has been shown to be an efficient way of improving the optical properties of CDs [16,17] and the use of Mn(II) ions as a doping source is regarded as an excellent archetype for the investigation of doping effects [18,19].

Further, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has been widely used and exhibited an excellent collaboration ability with fluorescence imaging [20]. Many kinds of fluorescence/MR bimodal CDs have been prepared using gadolinium as MR unit. However, gadolinium ions are highly toxic because of calcium channels inhibition [21,22]. Mn(II) ions exhibit low toxicity and have been reported as an efficient contrast agent for MRI [23], which would be a good choice to CDs for building new fluorescence/MR bimodal probes [24]. Therefore, the design of a CDs coordinated with Mn(II) ions for fluorescence enhancement and obtaining MR activity is highly desired.

Solvothermal method as a novel and simple method has become a mature technology for preparing nanomaterials. For example, Zhang et al. prepared PS@ZnO nanoparticles by two steps of fabrication of polystyrene (PS) spheres and fabrication of PS@ZnO in ethanol [25], and they also demonstrate a double-phase method for synthesizing the blue emitting ZnO nanoparticles that are stable in aqueous solutions [26]. Not only for semiconductor nanomaterials, solvothermal method is mostly preferred because of its simplicity, controlled reaction conditions, and cost-effectiveness for the preparation of CDs. Herein, we prepare Mn(II)-coordinated CDs (Mn-CDs) with fluorescence and MR bimodal properties via the one-pot solvothermal reaction of a mixture of p-phenylenediamine, ethanol, and MnCl2·4H2O at 180 °C for 4 h. We found that the fluorescence intensity and quantum yeild of the resulting CDs (Mn-CDs) were enhanced, and the quantum yield of the CDs increased greatly from 2.27% to 6.75%, meanwhile the CDs exhibited a higher MR activity (7.28 mM−1s−1) than that of commercial Gd-DTPA (4.63 mM−1s−1). The product could be separated by silica gel column chromatography into four fractions that emitted different colors from red to blue, which were denoted as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4, respectively. We found that the fluorescence emission of the CDs blue-shifted with the increase of particle size. After the characterization of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and inductively coupled plasma (ICP), Mn(II) was found to be centered on the yellow emitting CDs-2, which had high fluorescence and excellent magnetic resonance properties. White light-emitting CDs obtained by mixing CDs, had CIE coordinates of (0.37, 0.35). Notably, the white light emitting CDs showed solvent-dependent fluorescence emission, which can be used for the discrimination of organic solvents. Therefore, these Mn(II)-coordinated CDs can be used for rapid identification of organic solvents. This work not only offers a simple, cost-effective method of synthesizing fluorescence/MRI bimodal CDs with excellent fluorescence properties for the discrimination of organic solvents, but also promotes the development of metal-coordinated CDs in general (Scheme 1 ).

P-phenylenediamine was purchased from J&K Co., Beijing, China. Ethanol, methanol, n-propanol, n-butanol, petroleum ether, dichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran, N, N-dimethylformamide, petroleum ether and MnCl2·4H2O were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Co. Ltd. All of the reagents were used without any further purification.

Mn-CDs were prepared according to a previously CDs method with fine modification [27]. Briefly, 0.2 mmol of p-phenylenediamine and 0.4 mmol MnCl2·4H2O were added into 10 mL ethanol and stirred for 20 min. Then, the mixture was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The supernatants containing Mn-CDs were collected after centrifugation at 11000 rpm for 10 min. Four kinds of Mn-CDs with different fluorescence color were obtained via silica column chromatography separation using a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate as the eluent, and were denoted as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4, respectively.

TEM experiments were performed using a Tecnai 20 high resolution transmission electron microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 KeV. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB250 Xi. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with an UV–visible Agilent cray 100 spectrometer with a quartz cuvette. Infrared (IR) spectra were obtained with a Bruker Vector 22 spectrophotometer by using KBr pellets for solid samples from 400-4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The fluorescence spectra were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FS920P spectrometer. The inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) was performed using Optima 7300 V of Perkin Elmer. The magnetic resonance (MR) detections were performed with a 1.2 T MR imaging system (Huantong, Shanghai, China) according to a T1 detection program. X-ray diffraction patterns were taken on a Rigaku-Dmax 2500 diffractometer using Cu Ka1 radiation.

The test on quantum yield (QY) measurements were performed on the aforementioned fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments FS920P spectrometer), which was equipped with an integrating sphere. The fluorescence quantum yield of the sample was obtained according to the manual provided by Edinburgh Instrument. First, a solution of CDs was diluted with ethanol until the absorption intensity was less than 0.1 at the optimal excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Subsequently, this solution was added to a 5 mL fluorescence cuvette for QY measurements upon 365 nm monochromatic light excitation. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the ranges of 360–370 and 500–720 nm. The fluorescence spectra of ethanol were also recorded as a control test under identical conditions. Finally, fluorescence software was used to calculate the QYs based on the spectra of both the sample and the control. Each experiment was performed three times in parallel to obtain the average QY value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Mn(II)-coordinated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To date, a range of works have been reported that metals can influence the fluorescence properties of CDs [13,14]. We attempted to synthesize Mn(II)-coordinated CDs to investigate the influence of Mn coordination on fluorescence. Mn(II)-coordinated CDs were prepared by solvothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine and MnCl2·4H2O according to a previously reported method [27]. The as-prepared products were separated via silicon column chromatography using a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate as the eluent. The preparation and application of these CDs were illustrated in Scheme 1.

The fractions obtained by silica column chromatography showed different fluorescence characteristics, and were denoted as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4, respectively. As shown as Fig. 1 , four CDs had similar UV–vis absorption spectra. The peaks in the 200–350 nm region corresponded to the π-π* transitions of the C=C bonds [27]. The less intense peak that shifted from 380 to 420 nm is characteristic of CDs (Fig. 1) [27,28]. The four CDs did not exhibit the excitation-dependent fluorescence typically seen in previously reported CDs (Fig. 1) [29].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images indicated that all four CDs were monodisperse (Fig. 2 A–D), and they exhibited lattice spacing of 0.25 nm in high-resolution TEM images (Inlet of Fig. 2A–D), which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite [30]. The average diameters of the four CDs were 2.39, 3.28, 4.58, and 5.77 nm for CDs-1–4, respectively. In combination with the results of fluorescence (Fig. 1), these CDs exhibited size-dependent emission, with the fluorescence wavelength blue shifting as the size of CDs increased, similar to a previous report by Li et al. [31].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra shown in Fig. 3 A were carried out to characterize the chemical bonds and functional groups in the CDs. The four CDs exhibited similar IR spectra, indicating that the existence of similar chemical compositions. They showed absorption bands characteristic of amino (N-H) or hydroxyl (O-H) stretching vibrations at 3431 cm−1, the absorption from C-N at 1388 cm−1, C=N/C=O stretching at 1640 cm−1, and C-O-C absorption bands at 1100 cm−1, indicating the presence of these functional groups on the surface. An absorption peak at 1525 cm−1 in CDs-1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=C bonds, while the other CDs had no similar peaks. This indicates that the red emission of CDs-1 was related to an increase in sp2 domains [32]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the samples further. The full spectra were presented in Fig. S1, and show three typical peaks: C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (531 eV). In the high-resolution spectra (Fig. S2), the C 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to sp2 carbons (C=C, 284.7 eV), sp3 carbons (C-O/C-N, 286.1 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 288.8 eV). The N 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.4 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.2 eV, representing pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively.

The peaks at 532.2 eV in the O 1s spectra represent both C=O and C-O. The XPS analysis of these CDs further confirmed the results from the IR data. Notably, a Mn2p signal (642 eV) could be only found in the CDs-2 sample with an amount of 0.49%, confirming the existence of Mn(II) coordinated in CDs-2, which had abundant C-O-C and C=O bonds for doping. Further, Mn(II) content in CDs-2 as determined using HNO3 digestion and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) was 0.833 ppm. In XPS spectrum, the Mn2p spectrum is partially similar to that of MnO, demonstrating the presence of Mn⋯O coordination. However, it is noteworthy that the Mn⋯O coordination peak does not appear in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2), indicating that very few oxygen atoms participate in this doping process, which is consistent with the low absolute content of Mn(II) (<1 at.%) in CDs-2.

Mn(II) has magnetic resonance (MR) activity, and could be used to be a contrast agent for MR imaging [23]. The longitudinal (r1) of Mn(II)-coordinated CDs was measured with a 1.2 T MR imaging system at 30 °C. The r1 relaxivity curves showed an apparent enhancement of relaxation with the concentration of Mn(II) increased (Fig. 4 A). Gd-DTPA, a commercially available MRI agent was test to illustrate the MR performance of Mn(II)-coordinated CDs (Fig. 4B). The r1 relaxivity of CDs-2 was 7.28 mM−1s−1, higher than that of Gd-DTPA (4.63 mM−1s−1) under the same conditions (Fig. 4). The CDs had high hydrophilicity because of high oxygen content (12.57%, data from XPS). Proton in water molecules can easily accessed to the Mn(II) ions coordinated in the CDs and contributed to the high r1 relaxivity. Therefore, these Mn(II)-coordinated CDs have a potential as a fluorescence/MR bimodal probe. XRD analysis was carried out to analysis the structure of the CDs (CDs without Mn(II) and Mn-CDs) in Fig. S3. XRD spectroscopy of CDs without Mn(II) show amorphous peaks of carbonaceous backbone around 20–23°(2θ), whereas one superimposed reflection at around 19°(2θ) was observed in the pattern of Mn-CDs. Thus, the lattice spacing was calculated to be 0.25 nm, which was related to the (100) facet of graphite. We have also found Mn(II) presence as a form of Mn⋯O according to the result of XPS. Therefore, Mn(II)-coordination CDs can be successfully prepared in our method [15].

To investigate the effect of Mn(II) on the CDs, we analyzed the as-prepared fluorescent CDs obtained by one-pot solvothermal synthesis. The fluorescence intensity initially increased as the Mn(II) content increased, then decreased at an Mn(II) concentration of 0.4 mmol (Fig. 5 A and B). Moreover, the quantum yield gradually increased with increasing Mn(II)-coordinated (Fig. 5C). This demonstrates that Mn(II) doping has an impact on the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield. Further, a control test without of Mn(II) doping was performed. Three fractions (blue, green, and red emitting samples) were separated by column chromatography using a mixture of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate. In combination with the characterization of IR and XPS (Fig. 3A, Fig. S1 and Fig. S2), the surface functional groups of four CDs are identical, indicating that Mn(II) coordination affect the energy levels of sp2 core of CDs to change their emission wavelength. When the Mn-CDs were excited, photons were absorbed by Mn(II) through the transitions of 6A1→4T1, followed by energy transfer between Mn(II) and sp2 core [33]. The radiative recombination in the energy levels of sp2 core occurred to make the fluorescence enhanced, which was similar with Mn(II)-bonded reduced graphene oxide [33].

White emitting CDs were obtained from a mixture of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4. White light emission was observed upon irradiation at 302 nm (Fig. 6 A), and had CIE coordinates of (0.37, 0.35) (Fig. 6B), close to those of pure white light (0.33, 0.33). The fluorescence spectrum of the white light emitting CDs shows three distinct emission bands with maxima at λ = 381, 472, and 591 nm (Fig. 6A), this makes it suitable for applications in optical devices.

We found that when the mixture of CDs was dissolved in different organic solvents, different fluorescence patterns were obtained in each solvent upon irradiation at 302 nm (Fig. 7 A). Moreover, the different fluorescence colors could be obtained by solvent drying and addition (Fig. 7B). The fluorescence spectra of the mixed CDs in different solvents are shown in Fig. 7C. Three different fluorescence areas can be seen at 625, 550, and 450 nm, representing red, green, and blue fluorescence, respectively. Notably, methanol, ethanol, propanol, and n-butanol can be clearly distinguished using the CDs mixture as different colored fluorescence was observed in each of these four solvents (The dashed part in Fig. 7A). However, the single solutions of CDs (CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4 in this work) have no selectivity for different polar solvents. Only the fluorescence of mixture of CDs have discrimination in different solvents, due to the dominant carbon dots are different after mixing the four CDs in these organic solvents with different polarity and protonation, so that the fluorescence is different. Therefore, the single solutions of CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs-4 did not allow such distinction because anyone of these CDs have the same UV–vis absorption spectrum and same fluorescence color in different organic solvents, for example in all four solvents CDs-1 showed red fluorescence (Figs. S4 and S5). The principle of discrimination can be concluded as follows. We thought that the polarity and protonation of the solvents plays an important role in the color changes. The emission of the mixture, in particular the shorter wavelength component (CDs-3 and CDs-4) were in dominance when CDs dissolved in aprotic solvents with low polarity, thus exhibiting blue-green emitting emission. In contrast, the emission of the longer wavelength component (CDs-1 and CDs-2) appeared in protic solvents, thus red fluorescence gradually exhibited with the increase of polarity. For aprotic solvents with high polarity, such as DMF, a white emitting emission can be observed because the fluorescence of four CDs can be simultaneously expressed. Thus, the different color could be controlled by the fluorescence dominance of four CDs in different solvents. These CDs prepared from the solvothermal reaction between p-phenylenediamine and MnCl2·4H2O will be a useful tool for the simple discrimination of organic solvents [34].

We reported a simple one-pot solvothermal treatment of a mixture of p-phenylenediamine, ethanol, and MnCl2·4H2O to prepare Mn(II)-coordinated CDs with full-color emission that is independent of the excitation wavelength. Four types of CDs could be separated via column chromatography after the hydrothermal treatment, which was also beneficial for exploring the fluorescence mechanisms of the CDs. After being subjected to the same separation and purification, the products can be dispersed again in aqueous solution. We found that Mn(II) was primarily located in CDs-2. Monitoring the fluorescence intensity and quantum yeild demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs can be enhanced with Mn(II) doping, and the obtained CDs showed magnetic resonance properties. In addition, white light emitting CDs with CIE coordinates of (0.37, 0.35) were obtained by mixing various types of CDs, and could be used for the discrimination of organic solvents. The properties of the CDs were studied and were found to include improved quantum yield and fluorescence intensity, in addition to the aforementioned detection of organic solvents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21605036, 21771050 and 21271060), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. B2016202147, B2016202149, B2017202048 and B2017202068) and the Educational Committee of Hebei Province (No. QN2016172).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.02.026.
